title: A TV Show About Love and Money for Disabled People
summary: Kyla Harris is an actress who made a TV show called We Might Regret This. She has a boyfriend, but they don't live together because she might lose money from the government. This money helps her pay for things she needs because she is disabled. In the show, a character named Freya, who uses a wheelchair, faces the same problem with her boyfriend. The government gives money to disabled people based on their income, and if they live with a partner, they might get less. This makes it hard for disabled people to choose between love and having enough money. Kyla wants people to talk about this issue more because many don't think disabled people can be in relationships. A charity says disabled people need extra money each month to live like others. The government says there are other benefits that don't depend on income, but the rules can be confusing.

money from the government to help people pay for things they need
a boyfriend or girlfriend you are in a relationship with

background_read: This article is about a TV show that talks about a problem some disabled people face. Disabled people might get money from the government to help with daily life, like paying for care or equipment. This money is called benefits. If they live with a partner, the government might give them less money because it looks at both people's income together. This can make it hard for disabled people to choose to live with someone they love. The TV show wants to start conversations so more people understand this issue. It's important because everyone deserves love and support, and rules should not make it difficult for disabled people to have relationships.
Article_Structure: WHO: Kyla Harris, an actress and co-creator of a TV show, and disabled people who face money issues when living with partners.
WHAT: A TV show highlights how disabled people might lose government benefits if they move in with a partner, creating a choice between love and financial support.
WHERE: In the UK, as shown by government rules and a charity report.
WHY: To raise awareness about a little-known problem and encourage discussions so disabled people can have relationships without financial worries.

perspectives.description: She believes the rules are unfair and wants more people to talk about how disabled people face dilemmas in relationships due to benefit cuts.
They say there are other benefits available and encourage people to report changes, but the rules can still affect disabled households.
